# Git Concepts — Pull, Push, Merge & Divergent Branches
*Generated by Claude Code, 2026-06-09 — based on a real incident in this project*

## The Problem We Hit

After committing fixes locally on `updates`, `git push origin updates` was rejected:

```
! [rejected] updates -> updates (fetch first)
error: failed to push some refs to 'github.com:YUVARAJ-R-ai/wayline.git'
hint: Updates were rejected because the remote contains work that you do not have locally.
```

A teammate had pushed a new commit to `origin/updates` while we were working locally.
The histories had **diverged** — neither side was simply ahead of the other:

```
GitHub:  ...fd5242a → b096d3b           (remote got a new commit)
Local:   ...fd5242a → 834a852 → 805b009 (we added two commits)
```

Git refuses to push in this state because it would silently overwrite the remote commit.

---

## What `git pull origin updates` Does

Fetch the remote's version of `updates` and **integrate** it into your local branch.
It is a shorthand for two commands:
1. `git fetch origin updates` — download remote commits (no changes to working files)
2. `git merge origin/updates` — apply those commits to your local branch

Three integration strategies exist:

| Flag | Strategy | What happens |
|------|----------|-------------|
| `--no-rebase` | Merge | Creates a new "merge commit" joining both histories. Safe. |
| `--rebase` | Rebase | Replays your commits on top of the remote. Cleaner history, but risky when your commits delete files the remote modified. |
| `--ff-only` | Fast-forward only | Refuses unless one side is cleanly ahead. Would fail here. |

Git rejected the plain `git pull` with:
```
fatal: Need to specify how to reconcile divergent branches.
```
Because you hadn't told it which strategy to use. Fix: `git pull --no-rebase origin updates`

---

## Why We Used Merge, Not Rebase

The remote commit (`b096d3b`) modified `public/index.html` and `public/index.css`.
Our local commits **deleted** those same files (switching to the new microservice architecture).

- **Merge** resolves this as a "modify/delete" conflict — git picks the deletion (our side wins) and records a merge commit. Verified safe via `git merge-tree`.
- **Rebase** replays our deletions on top of the modification — this triggers an actual conflict marker that must be resolved manually.

Merge was the correct and safer choice.

---

## Why `git pull` ≠ `git merge`

These look similar but work from opposite directions:

| Command | What it integrates | Where commits come from |
|---------|-------------------|------------------------|
| `git pull origin updates` | Remote commits → local branch | GitHub (needs download) |
| `git merge updates` | A local branch → current branch | Already on your machine |

Since the missing commits existed only on GitHub, you had to `pull`.
`merge` would have had nothing new to integrate — those commits weren't downloaded yet.

---

## Safe Sequence When Branches Diverge

```bash
# 1. Check what the remote has that you don't (no changes made)
git fetch origin
git log updates..origin/updates --oneline

# 2. Simulate the merge to check for conflicts (no changes made)
git merge-tree --write-tree updates origin/updates

# 3. Pull with explicit merge strategy (creates a merge commit)
git pull --no-rebase --no-edit origin updates

# 4. Push the now-unified history
git push origin updates
```

`--no-edit` skips the editor for the merge commit message and uses the default.

---

## Checking if a Push Will Succeed Before Trying

```bash
git fetch origin
git log origin/updates..updates --oneline   # your local-only commits
git log updates..origin/updates --oneline   # remote-only commits (empty = safe to push)
```

If the second command returns nothing, your branch is ahead only — push will succeed.
If it returns commits, you need to pull first.
